title: Matrix Definitions and Types
summary: Comprehensive study of matrix notation, size, types, and the transpose operation.
content_html:
A matrix is a rectangular array of numbers arranged in rows and columns. We denote matrices with capital letters (A, B, C, ...).

Notation and Size

An m×n matrix has m rows and n columns. The element at row i, column j is written aij.

A = [a₁₁  a₁₂  a₁₃]   ← row 1
    [a₂₁  a₂₂  a₂₃]   ← row 2
    [a₃₁  a₃₂  a₃₃]   ← row 3  → 3×3 matrix

Special Matrix Types

  • Square matrix: m = n. E.g., A is 3×3.
  • Column vector: m×1 matrix (single column).
  • Row vector: 1×n matrix (single row).
  • Zero matrix (0): all elements are 0.
  • Identity matrix (I_n): n×n square matrix with 1s on main diagonal, 0s elsewhere.
  • Diagonal matrix: square matrix where all off-diagonal entries are 0.
  • Symmetric matrix: A = AT (element aij = aji for all i,j).
  • Upper triangular: aij = 0 for all i > j.
  • Lower triangular: aij = 0 for all i < j.
  • Orthogonal matrix: ATA = I (columns are orthonormal vectors).

Transpose

The transpose AT is formed by interchanging rows and columns: (AT)ij = Aji.

If A = [1  2  3]   then  A^T = [1  4]
       [4  5  6]               [2  5]
                               [3  6]

Transpose Properties

  • (AT)T = A
  • (A + B)T = AT + BT
  • (kA)T = kAT
  • (AB)T = BTAT (note reversed order)

Trace

The trace of a square matrix is the sum of all diagonal elements: tr(A) = a11 + a22 + ... + ann.
